ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific scallop fried rice entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GXM covers fried rice as a category; no scallop-specific fried rice code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for scallop fried rice. None assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of scallop fried rice is approximately 400 g, typically presented as a single plate with rice, scallops, egg, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice with Seafood — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO INFOODS SEA adapted with USDA FoodData Central scallop component
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for scallop fried rice exists in IMR MyFCD or FAO INFOODS SEA. Values are adapted from IMR MyFCD fried rice (seafood variant) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked scallops (FDC ID 175177) and plain fried rice (FDC ID 169752). Macros represent a weighted composite for a dish of approximately 55% cooked rice, 20% scallop, 10% egg, 10% oil/seasoning, 5% vegetables. Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Fried rice GI — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice, cooked, stir-fried)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for scallop fried rice. GI estimated at 55 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried rice (white rice base, GI range 48–56, representative value 55). The addition of scallop protein and egg is expected to slightly lower the effective GI relative to plain fried rice; GI 55 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving calculated as (55 × 22.5 × 4.0) / 100 = 49.5 g per 400 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid scallop fried rice due to its high sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake exceeding the 2000 mg recommended limit in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 55) and high glycemic load per serving (~49.5 g) from white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume scallop fried rice in small portions due to its high glycemic load driven by the white rice base; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and monitoring post-meal blood glucose is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content; scallops provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content (~1680 mg per serving) is a primary concern; however, scallops contribute lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids, so portion control and reduced-sodium preparation are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Scallop Fried Rice (扇贝炒饭; Nasi Goreng Kerang Kipas) is a Malaysian-style wok-fried rice dish featuring fresh or dried scallops as the primary protein, combined with day-old cooked white rice, egg, garlic, spring onion, soy sauce, and cooking oil. The dish is prepared using high-heat wok cooking (wok hei technique), which imparts a characteristic smoky, slightly charred aroma and flavour. The texture is fluffy yet slightly crispy at the edges, with tender scallop pieces providing a sweet, briny contrast to the savoury seasoned rice. In Singapore and Malaysia, this dish is commonly served at coffee shops (kopitiams), Chinese seafood restaurants, and hawker-style establishments. EC Coffee Bar on Carpenter Street, Singapore, offers a premium version priced at SGD 26.30, reflecting the use of quality scallops. Nutritionally, scallop fried rice provides moderate energy (~168 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein (~7.8 g per 100 g) from scallops and egg, and carbohydrates (~22.5 g per 100 g) from white rice. Sodium is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load per standard serving is high (~49.5 g), requiring caution for people with Type 2 Diabetes. Scallops contribute vitamin B12, zinc, and phosphorus, supporting neurological and bone health.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for scallop fried rice exists in IMR MyFCD or FAO INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest fried rice (seafood variant) entries in approved sources and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central scallop and fried rice components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach rather than a direct food match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases used (IMR MyFCD, FAO INFOODS SEA, USDA FoodData Central fried rice entry). Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases for this dish type. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases for this dish type. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in stir-fried rice dishes per USDA FoodData Central. GI set to 55 (low category per ≤55 threshold) based on analogous fried rice entry from University of Sydney GI Database; this is a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving = (55 × 22.5 × 4.0) / 100 = 49.5 g for a 400 g serving. Halal status is marked false as scallop fried rice may contain soy sauce with alcohol-based additives depending on preparation; restaurant-specific halal certification should be verified. The food_name has been normalised from the menu-style 'Ec Scallop Delight Fried Rice' to the canonical English form 'Scallop Fried Rice'. This dish is classified as Malaysian cuisine served in a Singapore kopitiam context; multilingual names follow the Singaporean dishes rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
